fr:title.text: Migratory-sharing optimization collapses read-then-write into one coherence transaction

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Migratory-sharing optimization collapses read-then-write into one coherence transaction

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A common access pattern is migratory: a core reads a line and then writes it — think a lock or a hand-off variable that passes from core to core. Handled naively this costs two cache-coherence transactions: a read miss that fetches the line shared, then a write miss that upgrades it to modified. Upgrade-miss detection spots the pattern and, on the read miss, transitions the line straight to a migratory-exclusive state, so the following write is silent — one transaction instead of two.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The same optimization hurts producer–consumer sharing, where one core writes and several read. There the right move on a read miss is to downgrade the previous writer rather than invalidate it, so later readers source the line from the last writer instead of racing to own it. The lesson generalizes: coherence optimizations are workload-shaped — a win for one sharing pattern is a loss for another.

fr:title.text: Directory-based coherence scales by replacing broadcasts with point-to-point messages

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Directory-based coherence scales by replacing broadcasts with point-to-point messages

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A scalable Cache coherence organization for systems too large for a shared bus. Each memory address has a home node; the home node maintains a directory entry listing which caches currently hold copies and in what state. Coherence transactions are point-to-point messages routed via the directory rather than broadcasts. The directory is itself distributed — each node owns the entries for the addresses in its local DRAM, and a physical address uniquely identifies its home — so there is no central bottleneck.
Compared to snoop-based coherence, directory protocols trade a level of indirection (3 hops instead of 2 for some transactions) for vastly reduced bandwidth — making them viable at hundreds of cores.

Three refinements make directory coherence practical. Its metadata is kept from scaling with memory by sparse directories; its multi-hop transactions force the protocol to reason about transient states and races; and workload-specific patterns like migratory sharing let it collapse pairs of transactions into one. See Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi for a full worked implementation in Murphi.

In a Multicore system where every core has its own Cache hierarchy, the same memory address can live in multiple caches simultaneously. A cache coherence protocol is the mechanism that keeps those copies consistent — propagating writes, invalidating stale copies, and preserving two invariants:

html:ol.html:li: Single-writer invariant: at any moment, at most one core has permission to write a given line. No other core can read or write it concurrently.
Data-value invariant: all cores see the same order of updates to a given line.

html:ul.html:li: snoop-based coherence — every cache watches a shared bus and reacts to other cores' traffic. Scales to ~8 cores.
directory-based coherence — a structure tracks who has a copy of each line; coherence messages are point-to-point. Scales to hundreds of cores at the cost of an extra hop.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The concrete state machines are protocols like MSI protocol, MESI protocol, MOESI protocol, and DEC Firefly, each adding states to handle more cases or to optimize specific sharing patterns. A worked implementation of directory-based MESI in Murphi lives in Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi.
Worked instance: Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi implements directory-based MESI in Murphi.
